posts.cooked:
Curious if anyone has ever used their wine cellar space for aging food products?

I’d really love to experiment with curing and aging a ham or producing prosciutto (duck and pork both!) and am curious if anyone has used their cellar for this kind of project and how it worked out?

Any other projects in this vein people have been successful with?

posts.name: Bri@n Kelly
posts.cooked: Very interested as I had the exact same though occur to me the other day.

posts.name: Rob Isaacs
posts.cooked: I’ve aged homemade bressola with great results. Also use it as a place to ripen cheese.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I was curious about the prosciutto part.

The Prosciutto di Parma consorzio requires three to four weeks at low refrigerator temperature (under 38F) and high humidity (80%):

Prosciutto Di Parma

What is Prosciutto? - History of Prosciutto | Parmacrown.com

What is prosciutto and what sets Prosciutto di Parma apart from all of the rest? Find out why Prosciutto di Parma is known as the King of Italian Ham.

Est. reading time: 4 minutes

But this article makes it sound like wine cellar conditions could be ideal (55F, 65% RH):

gastrochemist.com

Prosciutto

Also, having visited a prosciutto plant south of Parma a few years ago, at least in big quantities, the aging hams stink to high heaven. It was nauseating.

Seems like a wine cellar could be ideal for aging cheese. I store olive oil and beer in my cellar, but that’s a very prosaic use.

link_counts.title: What is Prosciutto? - History of Prosciutto | Parmacrown.com

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I’m guessing most of us are like me in that I have the polar opposite issue from “what to do with unused surplus wine cellar space.”

But it is an interesting question, carry on.

posts.name: Brandon Redman
posts.cooked: I grow lots of onions and, for most of them, I leave them in ground into the fall and then “cure” them for long-term storage. They’re down in the wine cellar.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: If you are interested to do so, can you say a bit about how long you do that and how that affects the onions? Is it mostly just to store them, or does it actually change their character in some desirable way?

posts.name: brad dehn
posts.cooked: What type of onions, and is the higher humidity level OK with the curing process?

posts.name: Eric White
posts.cooked: yes, I’ve cured meat in my wine cellar, conditions are great for it. Much better than the fridge conversion I used before the cellar was finished.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
EricZ:

if I had a walk in wine fridge, I’d be all over:

https://www.costco.com/covap jamon-iberico-bellota-ham-leg-with-stand-and-knife%2C-15.4-lbs. .product.100519895.html

I had this exact Jamon in my wine cellar for 2 months before before breaking it out for New Years!
FYI, the provided knife is near useless.

posts.name: Brandon Redman
posts.cooked:
Chris / Brad, I do it almost solely for long-term storage. I know onions are cheap and plentiful at the grocery store, but I just love growing some of my own stuff and, when onions are ready, having a TON of them at once isn’t ideal. As such, I cure them and store them to use until the following summer. The cellar is very cool and dark, obviously.

While I don’t remember the exact variety off-hand, they’re not “sweet” onions but, rather, some variety of standard “yellow” onion. From what I’ve read, really high sugar sweet onions don’t store as well. To cure them properly, leave them in the ground until the green tops flop over and start to brown. Once they’re pretty thoroughly shriveled around the neck, pull them out of the ground and lay them out in a cool area with all of the leaves / tops still intact. Over a couple of weeks, those tops will shrivel even more but, most importantly, the onion skin will dehydrate and tighten considerably. Once they’re done, cut off those tops and store in the wine cellar. I just had another this past weekend and it was like new.

posts.name: Rob Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
I’ve now done Guanciale in the wine cellar (my cellar is typically 53-55F and 80-90% humidity) a few times. Last batch was finishing in the height of this winter, when the humidity was 95%+ (due to unit not running much - my natural humidity is 100%) and did get a bunch of mold on it, but after taking the (harmless looking) mold off the Guanciale came out great, and I’ve lived to tell the tale.

Trying a coppa in the cellar now that we’re heading into Spring and my humidity is back in the 80s%.

posts.name: Patchen Markell
posts.cooked: I’ve occasionally used a Eurocave to ripen whole, store-bought washed-rind cheeses that feel too firm when purchased. Works very well… if you remember they’re there.

posts.name: Brian Gilp
posts.cooked: I have cured Saucisson in my cellar. My cellar is passive and conditions are only right for a few months. It is really easy and not terribly expensive to turn and old refrigerator into a curing chamber so did that and do most of my curing there now.

when we visited Willett in Kentucky, Sean Brock had hams hanging in their aging warehouses. have wanted to do something similar since!

I modified a recipe from The New Charcuterie Cookbook — Jamie Bissonnette. I bought it used for $2.

Lots of info on the web that can get you started.

posts.name: Eric White
posts.cooked:
I tend to follow Rimmerman’s book for the important stuff (salt, curing salt percentages), but wing it entirely on the spices

Edit to add - I tend toward whole muscle cures, such as coppa. I’ve tried a whole leg a couple times, but sadly failed.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My cellar is at 80% humidity, which is a touch high for long term storage of hams in my experience, but works very well for cigars even if some would consider it a touch high.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
Somewhat similar thread on this topic several years ago (wouldn’t have been easy to find unless you recalled the conversation, which I did… I probably need a life )

What else do you keep in your wine cellar? WINE TALK
With us buying in greater quantities to minimize trips to the store (or deliveries), I find things other than wine are finding a home in the wine cellar. While my wife has always stored her Cannoli shells in the cellar, and we keep the “good” olive oil in there, it now also contains vacuum-packed coffee beans and nuts. I think the potatoes may do well in there too, but I’m reluctant because the bags aren’t sealed like the nuts and coffee. What, besides wine, do other folks keep in their cellars? …

link_counts.title: What else do you keep in your wine cellar?

posts.name: Dave McIsaac
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

My cellar is at 80% humidity, which is a touch high for long term storage of hams in my experience, but works very well for cigars even if some would consider it a touch high.

There was (is?) a cigar shop in Cleveland (Cousins) that kept the humidor very cool (cold) and very high humidity. They did this to kill off/prevent any cigar beetles. Assuming your cellar is around 55 degrees, 80% humidity would be just about perfect for cigar storage (temp + humidity = 140)

posts.cooked: You don’t really need a wine cellar for that. I got one a few years ago and it just sat on my counter for a couple of months while we worked our way through it.

posts.name: Greg Pierce
posts.cooked: I am just amazed that folks have such ginormous wine cellars that allow them to do stuff other than store wine. Mine is so stuffed that it routinely takes a fair amount of time moving stuff out so that I can get to any given wine I’m looking for.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: I keep chocolate in the wine cellar and hide Mounds bars there also.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Mattstolz:
John_Morris:

Also, having visited a prosciutto plant south of Parma a few years ago, at least in big quantities, the aging hams stink to high heaven. It was nauseating.

have done the same, and honestly, I loved that smell! not as much as the parmesan aging room, but still enjoyed it.

In September 2016, by dumb luck, we arrived in Langhirano for the annual Festa del Prosciutto. I’d tried to set up a tour with an artisanal producer, but the tourist office sent us to an enormous industrial plant owned by an Austrian company:

P10301111920×1281 290 KB

When we were there, it was the 50th anniversary of the 1966 festival, for which the locals constructed an enormous (paper mache?) ham – 25 to 30 feet long, I’m estimating – that they hung over the road at the entrance to town. Photos of that faux ham were all over the town in 2016.

P10301151920×1281 250 KB

P10301141920×2877 291 KB

posts.name: Jim Freeman
posts.cooked: My cellar doubles as my wife’s fur storage.

posts.name: Brandon Redman
posts.cooked: Don’t be. I keep maybe 100 bottles at my home in my cellar with most of my wine at an offsite. It’s a passive cellar that, partly due to my home being in the Pacific NW of the USA, stays rock-solid at around 55-65 year-round. There’s more than enough room for a pile of onions!

posts.name: David Fischer
posts.cooked: I use my wine cellar when I make lemoncello (I use a method that involves suspending the lemons in a large jar over the liquor for two months). I have also used it when fermenting items (albeit in a jar with a water lock).

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
When we host Thanksgiving or Christmas we’re generally feeding 12-20 people a couples meals per day.

The wine room becomes overflow for things that need cool, but not cold: pies, eggs, hearty produce, cheese & etc.

posts.name: T h o mas M e ll o ni
posts.cooked:
I have used my cellar for storage of onions, garlic and hot peppers. I also made some dried sausage (salami). Humidity was not as high as I would like. Generally, around 70% is the recommended level for making cured meats. I was around 55%. The dried sausage/salami was good. I just needed a slower and longer dry time.
